questions.question: What is the main problem with paging on every infrastructure threshold breach?
options: It often wakes people for low-signal symptoms that may not reflect user harm.
It makes CPU usage impossible to measure.
It guarantees lower MTTR for every outage.
It prevents any use of SLOs.
questions.explanation: Raw threshold pages often create noise without clear urgency.

questions.question: Why do teams separate pages, tickets, and informational alerts?
options: Because not every issue requires immediate human interruption, and operator attention is limited.
Because metrics systems can only deliver one alert per day.
Because ticketing systems replace dashboards completely.
Because pages are only legal for storage systems.
questions.explanation: Severity routing protects attention and matches response urgency to actual risk.

questions.question: What is a strong reason to page on SLO burn rate instead of only on CPU or memory?
options: It reflects meaningful user harm more directly and reduces noisy infrastructure-only wakeups.
It guarantees every incident will be root-caused automatically.
It removes the need for runbooks and ownership.
It makes dependency failures impossible.
questions.explanation: SLO burn is closer to the actual reliability promise made to users.

questions.question: Why combine short-window and long-window burn alerts?
options: To catch both sudden severe incidents and slower ongoing degradation.
To ensure every alert pages two engineers by default.
To remove the need for any dashboard review.
To keep request volume constant through the week.
questions.explanation: Different windows catch different failure shapes.

questions.question: What is a common failure mode in alert design?
options: One underlying dependency issue pages many downstream teams independently with little context.
The runbook contains a title and an owner.
The service exposes region-level metrics.
The alert message includes a dashboard link.
questions.explanation: Duplicate pages from one shared cause create confusion and fatigue.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with aggressive alert suppression or deduplication?
options: It reduces noise, but can hide genuinely distinct incidents if designed poorly.
It makes every page more severe automatically.
It guarantees zero missed incidents.
It prevents MTTR from being measured.
questions.explanation: Suppression helps attention, but bad suppression can bury important signals.

questions.question: Which alert is more likely to be ticket-worthy than page-worthy?
options: A capacity trend showing disk usage will become risky next week if nothing changes.
A fast burn of the primary request-success SLO right now.
A sudden regional outage affecting live customer traffic.
A fleet-wide spike in request failures above the emergency threshold.
questions.explanation: Trend-based issues usually merit planned work rather than immediate interruption.

questions.question: What is the strongest answer to 'What should every page contain?'
options: Enough context to assess impact quickly, plus ownership and the first action or runbook link.
Only the raw metric name so responders can infer the rest.
A full dump of all recent logs from every service.
The names of all engineers who ever touched the codebase.
questions.explanation: A useful page points responders toward immediate understanding and action.
